figures.caption: Fig. 1: Overall Architecture of DeTAiL. (a) In the distillation and GRPO stages, the MLLM learns to generate both the cognitive label and the textual rationale that explains its prediction; (b) in the MLP adaptor stage, a small MLP classifier is trained on the hidden representation of the MLLM given the prompt and the generated rationale.
TABLE I: Prompt template for generating evidence-based explanations conditioned on a gold cognitive-status label.

TABLE II: In-domain dementia classification results on ADReSS. For DeTAiL, “teacher” denotes conditioning on rationales generated generated by Gemini3.5-Flash, whereas “self” denotes conditioning on rationales generated by the adapted MLLM itself. Acc. denotes accuracy. Boldface and underlining denotes the best and second-best results, respectively. Models marked with “*” uses average result over multiple seeds for the metrics and the equal-error-rate threshold for accuracy.

TABLE III: In-domain dementia classification results on LEADS with 10-fold cross validation (CV). We report two-class AUC, CI-only AUC for fine-grained CI discrimination, and three-class AUC. For DeTAiL, “teacher” denotes conditioning the MLLM on rationales generated by Gemini3.5-Flash, whereas “self” denotes conditioning on the rationale generated by the adapted MLLM itself. “*” denotes results with a different CV split from ours.
Fig. 2: Effect of MLLM layer choice for nonlinear adaptation on LEADS. We evaluate Qwen2.5-Omni-7B representations extracted from different layers with an MLP adaptor.
Fig. 3: Reliability of the most frequent evidence types in the rationale for DeTAiL on ADReSS. Reliability is estimated by computing the percentage of correct predictions using a given evidence.
